paper_title: Memory-Augmented Vision-Language Agents for Persistent and Semantically Consistent Object Captioning

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) often yield inconsistent descriptions of the same object across viewpoints, hindering the ability of embodied agents to construct consistent semantic representations over time. Previous methods resolved inconsistencies using offline multi-view aggregation or multi-stage pipelines that decouple exploration, data association, and caption learning, with limited capacity to reason over previously observed objects. In this paper, we introduce a unified, memory-augmented Vision-Language agent that simultaneously handles data association, object captioning, and exploration policy within a single autoregressive framework. The model processes the current RGB observation, a top-down explored map, and an object-level episodic memory serialized into object-level tokens, ensuring persistent object identity and semantic consistency across extended sequences. To train the model in a self-supervised manner, we collect a dataset in photorealistic 3D environments using a disagreement-based policy and a pseudo-captioning model that enforces consistency across multi-view caption histories. Extensive evaluation on a manually annotated object-level test set, demonstrate improvements of up to +11.86% in standard captioning scores and +7.39% in caption self-similarity over baseline models, while enabling scalable performance through a compact scene representation. Code, model weights, and data are available at https://hsp-iit.github.io/epos-vlm/.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 0 3 ] . [ 2 7 5 2 4 2 . 3 0 6 2 : r Memory-Augmented VisionLanguage Agents for Persistent and Semantically Consistent Object Captioning Tommaso Galliena1,2, Stefano Rosa1, Tommaso Apicella1, Pietro Morerio1, Alessio Del Bue1, and Lorenzo Natale1 1 Italian Institute of Technology, Genoa, Italy 2 University of Genoa, Genoa, Italy Abstract. VisionLanguage Models (VLMs) often yield inconsistent descriptions of the same object across viewpoints, hindering the ability of embodied agents to construct consistent semantic representations over time. Previous methods resolved inconsistencies using offline multi-view aggregation or multi-stage pipelines that decouple exploration, data association, and caption learning, with limited capacity to reason over previously observed objects. In this paper, we introduce unified, memory-augmented VisionLanguage agent that simultaneously handles data association, object captioning, and exploration policy within single autoregressive framework. The model processes the current RGB observation, topdown explored map, and an object-level episodic memory serialized into object-level tokens, ensuring persistent object identity and semantic consistency across extended sequences. To train the model in self-supervised manner, we collect dataset in photorealistic 3D environments using disagreement-based policy and pseudo-captioning model that enforces consistency across multi-view caption histories. Extensive evaluation on manually annotated object-level test set, demonstrate improvements of up to +11.86% in standard captioning scores and +7.39% in caption self-similarity over baseline models, while enabling scalable performance through compact scene representation. Code, model weights, and data are available at https://hsp-iit.github.io/epos-vlm/.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Humans actively construct robust visual understanding through dynamic, embodied exploration. From early infancy, perception is tightly coupled with action: learners build stable object representations by moving, approaching, rotating objects, and revisiting them over time [20,28,35]. Therefore, semantic consistency emerges from memory and repeated observations across diverse viewpoints rather than being an inherent property of independent visual inputs [28]. In contrast, modern vision-language models (VLMs) are predominantly trained and evaluated on static datasets. Although methods such as BLIP-2 [14], QwenVL [39], and LLaVA [17] achieve state-of-the-art captioning performance on single images, they exhibit severe limitations when deployed in embodied environments, 2 T.Galliena et al. Fig. 1: Memory-driven multi-view exploration progressively resolves ambiguous object captions into consistent object-level description. (a) the agent predicts caption for the observed object; (b) different caption is predicted from different viewpoint; (c) consistent caption is predicted based on the episodic object memory; (d) the predicted caption for the object remains consistent. including semantic inconsistency across viewpoints, lack of object permanence, and limited ability to integrate information over time. When an agent equipped with VLM navigates 3D scene, changes in viewpoint, distance, and occlusion frequently cause the same physical object to be described in inconsistent or contradictory ways [8]. This semantic drift breaks object identity over time and undermines the use of language as persistent representation for embodied reasoning, mapping, and decision-making. Prior research addressed semantic inconsistency across viewpoints through multi-view supervision [9], post-hoc aggregation [5], or combination of both [8], decoupling exploration, data association, captioning, and learning. In these frameworks, exploration is either confined to offline data collection or not explicitly modeled. Consequently, agents cannot adapt viewpoint selection based on the inconsistency of their current predictions, precluding correction of observational or aggregation errors through subsequent exploration. This decoupling fundamentally constrains the systems capability to actively acquire informative views that resolve ambiguities in object identity and attributes, particularly in novel environments. In contrast, we propose unified formulation where semantic consistency is optimized end-to-end during embodied navigation. Our exploration policy directly selects viewpoints to enhance fine-grained object semantics, enabling active resolution of perceptual ambiguities during navigation (see Fig. 1). We introduce EPOS-VLM (Embodied Persistent Object Semantics), unified Vision-Language model for embodied navigation, fine-tuned end-to-end for joint data association, object-level captioning, and action prediction. structured episodic memory stores per-object caption histories and 3D positions. This Memory-Augmented VisionLanguage Agents 3 memory is tokenized, allowing the pretrained VLM backbone to reason over longhorizon object histories within single autoregressive sequence. During training, the agent explores 3D environments, accumulating multi-view object observations. Object-level pseudo-captions, aggregated from memory histories, supervise finetuning of the same VLM. Joint learning of captioning, data association, and action selection yields policies that revisit semantically unstable objects and collect maximally informative viewpoints for ambiguity resolution. Our contributions include: (1) unified, memory-conditioned Vision-LanguageAction model specifically designed for embodied captioning, which jointly learns data association, object-level captioning, and action prediction; (2) structured tokenization of episodic object memory to enable end-to-end reasoning over long-horizon object histories within pretrained VLM; and (3) an embodied captioning dataset containing navigation data and object-level pseudo-captions, along with manually-annotated benchmark of object-level captions.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: We contextualize our contributions within three interconnected research areas: cross-view semantic consistency in embodied captioning, memory mechanisms for object-level representation, and action-driven exploration for semantic learning. Cross-View Semantic Consistency in Captioning. While modern VisionLanguage Models (VLMs) achieve strong performance on standard image captioning benchmarks [1, 14, 40], they are trained on independent image-text pairs and lack explicit mechanisms to enforce semantic consistency across observational variants (e.g., viewpoint shifts, occlusions, or scale changes). Consequently, embodied agents using off-the-shelf VLMs produce inconsistent object-level descriptions under environmental dynamicsa limitation not exposed by static image captioning evaluations [4]. Recent work addresses this limitation through post-hoc aggregation: ECO-score [13] selects captions via semantic and CLIPbased similarity, while IC3 [5] and [8] synthesize view-consistent descriptions using consensus or frequency-based in-context learning. Critically, these approaches only perform aggregation [5] or decouple aggregation from captioner training [8], treating consistency as an emergent property rather than an integrated objective. Our work bridges this gap by unifying cross-view consistency into the captioning process via end-to-end optimization. Object-level Memory for Embodied Representation. Memory augmentation has proven effective for long-horizon embodied reasoning, with frameworks like Mem2Ego [43] and MemoryVLA [27] leveraging episodic memory to resolve partial observability in navigation and manipulation. Similarly, 3DLLM-Mem [12] maintains spatial-temporal memory for persistent scene understanding. However, these systems optimize memory for task execution (e.g., path planning or action prediction), not to create accurate and consistent object descriptions. Additionally, these methods treat memory retrieval and caption generation as sequential stages, inhibiting joint optimization of data association and description synthesis. In contrast, our framework embeds object-level memory directly into the VLM 4 T.Galliena et al. architecture, enabling co-optimization of cross-view aggregation, data association, and autoregressive caption generation. Recent work has explored structured object-level tokenization to align 3D representations with language and vision in unified autoregressive framework [24]. Inspired by this object-level tokenization paradigm, our framework embeds episodic object memory directly into the captioning architecture, enabling joint optimization of cross-view aggregation, data association, and autoregressive caption generation. Action-Driven Semantic Learning. Active exploration strategies can enhance semantic consistency by strategically gathering informative observations. Systems like CaBOT [10] and Explore-and-Explain [4] demonstrate that viewpoint selection impacts caption informativeness, while Where to Learn [32] formalizes exploration policies for visual concept acquisition. Galliena et al. [8] further demonstrate that caption disagreement can be used as an intrinsic signal to guide exploration and distill object-level pseudo-captions in self-supervised setting. Nevertheless, existing methods decouple exploration from captioning: policies are either heuristic-based [10] or trained independently of the captioner [32], preventing online operation where evolving semantic uncertainty guides future observations. Our approach uniquely integrates action selection, data association, and captioning into single end-to-end framework, where uncertainty directly informs exploration to maximize cross-view consistency.

sections.title: 3 Embodied Persistent Object Semantics Visual Language
sections.content: Model (EPOS-VLM) We propose single memory-conditioned policy to explore an environment and create consistent language representation for each encountered object. An embodied agent navigates an environment containing set of objects = {oi}N i=1 over time steps {1, . . . , }, processing an RGB-D observation It RHW 4 from its current viewpoint and an RGB top-down explored map Et RHW 3 encoding visited regions. Since objects may be observed multiple times with varying appearances, the agent maintains an episodic object memory that assigns persistent identifiers to encountered objects and accumulates their spatial and semantic information. As shown in Fig. 2, EPOS-VLM conditions on It, Et, and Mt1 to predict: (1) data associations linking current detections to existing object IDs or initializing new ones, (2) object-level captions for observed instances, and (3) navigation action at A, where is discrete action set (e.g., move_forward, turn_left). The objective is to learn function Φθ(It, Et, Mt1) that predicts spatially consistent, viewpoint-invariant captions co for objects visible in It, while jointly predicting navigation actions at that maximize object knowledge acquisition.

sections.title: 3.1 Visual Observations and Object Identifiers
sections.content: From observation It, set of object instances is extracted using an instance segmentation model, yielding collection of detections {bj R4 j=1, where bj }Kt , pj Memory-Augmented VisionLanguage Agents Fig. 2: Overview of the proposed EPOS-VLM model. Structured episodic memory, RGB observations (with detected object bounding boxes and IDs overlayed), and top-down maps are jointly encoded and processed by VisionLanguage transformer, which autoregressively outputs object association, object-level captions, and actions. denotes the bounding box of the j-th instance, pj R3 its the estimated global 3D position obtained with depth projection and camera pose, and Kt denotes the number of detected objects. To enable explicit object-level reasoning within an autoregressive vision-language model, each detected instance is assigned transient object identifier rj N. Transient IDs are randomly sampled at each time step, do not correspond to persistent object identities contained in the episodic memory and are rendered directly onto the RGB observation alongside the corresponding bounding boxes. This visual annotation allows the model to unambiguously refer to individual detections when producing data association decisions or object-level captions when multiple detections are present in the image. The task of linking detections across time and maintaining persistent object identity is handled explicitly through the episodic memory mechanism described in the following section, rather than being inferred implicitly from visual appearance alone.

sections.title: 3.2 Episodic Object Memory Representation and Tokenization
sections.content: The episodic object memory provides persistent representation of previously observed physical objects and enables long-horizon reasoning within standard autoregressive Vision-Language model. Formally, at time step the episodic memory is represented as set of object entries Mt = {oid,o, pt,o, Ct,o}oOt where oid,o denotes persistent object identifier assigned to physical object, pt,o R3 is the estimated position of the object, and Ct,o stores the set of 6 T.Galliena et al. captions previously generated for the object together with their occurrence counts, capturing how often specific descriptions have been produced for the same object during exploration. The position pt,o is updated whenever new observation is associated with object using depth-based projection and camera pose, while Ct,o is updated by either incrementing caption count for if it already contains the predicted caption or by inserting new caption for o. To condition pretrained Vision-Language Model on episodic memory without architectural modifications, the memory Mt is serialized into structured textual representation and injected directly into the language context. Each object entry is encoded as contiguous block of text using special tokens: [OBJ] oid,o [POS] (ˆx, ˆy, ˆz) [CAPTIONS] {(ci, fi)}L i=1, (1) where (ˆx, ˆy, ˆz) denotes discretized version of the objects global position pt,o mapped to numeric tokens, and each pair (ci, fi) represents previously generated caption ci and the number of times fi it has been produced for that object. is the number of unique captions predicted for oid. All object blocks are concatenated and enclosed between the scene-level delimiters [SCENE-START] and [SCENE-END].

sections.title: 3.3 Data Association
sections.content: t }Kt At each time step t, the agent observes set of object detections indexed by transient identifiers {rj j=1 (Sec. 3.1) and maintains an episodic memory Mt containing entries indexed by persistent object identifiers {oid} (Sec. 3.2). The goal of data association is to determine, for each current detection, whether it corresponds to an existing memory entry or represents previously unseen physical object. Data association is performed explicitly through structured [MATCH] tokens generated by the autoregressive model as part of its output sequence. Each [MATCH] token establishes symbolic link between transient object identifier in the current RGB observation and persistent object identifier stored in episodic memory. Concretely, for each transient identifier rj , the model emits decision of the form [MATCH] rj oid,o or [MATCH] rj [NEW_ID], (2) where mapping to an existing oid,o indicates that the detection is associated with previously observed object, while the special token [NEW_ID] signals that the detection corresponds to novel physical object not yet present in memory. When detection is matched to an existing persistent identifier oid,o, the corresponding memory entry is updated using the current observation, refining the objects estimated position pt,o and updating the stored caption statistics Ct,o. When [NEW_ID] is emitted, new memory entry is created and initialized from the current detection, assigning fresh persistent identifier and storing its initial spatial and semantic information. Memory-Augmented VisionLanguage Agents Crucially, persistent object identifiers are never predicted directly from raw visual appearance. Instead, identity assignment emerges from joint reasoning over the current visual evidence, spatial proximity, and the semantic information accumulated in episodic memory. By integrating [MATCH] tokens into the same autoregressive stream that produces captions and actions, EPOS-VLM jointly learns object linking, semantic aggregation, and long-horizon identity maintenance within single unified policy.

sections.title: 3.4 Training EPOS-VLM
sections.content: Pseudo-caption generation. To supervise viewpoint-invariant object semantics, we aggregate multi-view observations from exploration episodes into single object-level pseudo-caption co per physical object using novel 3D-grounded pseudo-captioning method, 3D-Aware Consistent Pseudo-Captioner (3D-CPS), designed to produce view-consistent object descriptions. This process leverages two complementary sources: caption history and geometrically grounded visual observations, producing compact descriptions of object-intrinsic attributes while filtering spurious visual cues from occlusions or background clutter. We store all captions generated for object during exploration in captionfrequency dictionary Co that estimates descriptions consistency across viewpoints: correct attributes appear frequently, while view-specific or erroneous descriptions occur sporadically. Co thus serves as semantic prior for viewpoint-robust linguistic attributes. Using accumulated 3D points for object o, we estimate its spatial extent and select object-level image crops that maximize surface coverage of the reconstructed object. Candidate crops are ranked by distinct 3D region coverage and viewpoint diversity, prioritizing complementary views that expose defining attributes while minimizing redundancy and occlusion. An external vision-language model generates pseudo-caption co using (i) the cross-view caption frequency table Co and (ii) the geometrically selected image crops. The model summarizes only multi-view supported attributes, explicitly omitting perspective-dependent details (e.g., relative position or camera perspective). This yields concise pseudo-caption capturing object-intrinsic properties for self-supervised training. Fine-tuning. The object-level pseudo-captions co generated by 3D-CPS are then used as self-supervised targets to train EPOS-VLM: for every frame in which object is observed, the model is supervised to generate co (rather than view-specific caption), enforcing single, viewpoint-invariant description per object across the episode. We fine-tune pretrained vision-language model into unified autoregressive policy Φθ that jointly performs data association, object-level captioning, and action selection. At time step t, the model conditions on the unified context Xt = [It, Et, Mt1]. The policy autoregressively generates structured output sequence Yt = (cid:2)[MATCH] Πt [CAPTION] Ct [ACTION] at (cid:3), (3) 8 T.Galliena et al. with Πt encoding data association (matching memory IDs or emitting [NEW_ID]), Ct providing object-level captions, and at specifying navigation action. Parameters θ are optimized by minimizing the cross-entropy loss over the token sequence, i.e., maximizing the conditional likelihood of the target structured outputs given the context Xt. The supervision spans data association tokens, caption tokens, and action tokens within single unified sequence. By enforcing correct object linking, semantic consistency, and coherent actions within single autoregressive stream, the model to jointly processes object identity, semantic evidence, and action outcomes, rather than treating subtasks as separable stages.

sections.title: 4 Experimental Setup
sections.content: We evaluate EPOS-VLM across five complementary tasks: (i) object-level pseudocaption accuracy, (ii) object-level caption accuracy, (iii) cross-view semantic consistency, (iv) data association and computational scalability, and (v) impact of the action policy on semantic learning. This assessment determines whether the model improves captioning performance while maintaining persistent object semantics through embodied navigation. Data Collection. Simulations enable repeatable evaluation of long-horizon embodied behavior with access to depth, pose, and instance identifiersessential for object-level data association and consistency metrics, which remain challenging to obtain at scale in real-world settings. We conduct experiments in Habitat [26] using HM3D [21] and Gibson [37]. Scene-level splits follow the setup of Galliena et al. [8], but episodes are capped at 400 steps: HM3D training set consists of 128 training scenes, 27 validation scenes, and 28 test scenes. To assess out-ofdomain generalization, we evaluate the model trained on HM3D on manually annotated object-level test set from Gibson [37], comprising 32 scenes. To collect the training supervisory signals of EPOS-VLM, an heuristic-based exploration policy targets objects that exhibit high cross-view caption disagreement, and pseudo-captioner assigns to each object instance unique concise caption. The disagreement computation follows the methodology of Galliena et al. [8]: Mask2Former detector [7] with Swin backbone [18] (COCO-pretrained [16]) predicts instance segmentation masks and bounding boxes from input images; an image captioner (Qwen3-VL-2B [39]) predicts captions for each bounding box; semantic point cloud created using the agents pose and depth images embeds the segmentations and captions information; 3D object instances are segmented via connected components algorithm [29], and an object disagreement is computed as average cosine distance between all embeddings belonging to the object. The disagreement is then down-projected onto the ground plane, forming 2D disagreement map. We sample candidate viewpoints around high-disagreement regions at varying distances and orientations from each object and ranked by disagreement. Finally, training trajectories are collected while the agent navigates to each candidate viewpoint. Given that pseudo-captions are generated offline at the end of the exploration, we use larger model (Qwen3-VL-30B [39]). We report additional details in Supp. Mat. Memory-Augmented VisionLanguage Agents 9 Training details. We fine-tune EPOS-VLM for 3 epochs using AdamW, learning rate of 2 104, weight decay 0.01, and linear warmup over 3% of the training steps. Training is performed on 2 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with per-device batch size of 16 and gradient accumulation of 4 steps, with gradient clipping at max norm of 1.0 for stability. We adopt LoRA for efficient fine-tuning; the LoRA configuration is reported in the Supp. Mat. Methods under comparison. We evaluate pseudo-captioning performance against IC3 [5], ECO-score [13] and LD-CPS [8]. IC3 aggregates multi-view captions via LLM to capture comprehensive details; ECO-score ranks captions using semantic similarity and CLIP consensus; LD-CPS generates object-level pseudo-captions via LLM using agent-predicted captions and frequency. For object-level captioning, we benchmark against state-of-the-art Vision-Language Models: Qwen3-VL-2B [39], BLIP-2 [14], InternVL-3B [34], CoCa [40], and Florence [41], and LD-CPS+CLA pipeline [8] for cross-view consistency. We compare data association performance between our episodic object memory and Galliena et al. [8]s point-cloud based method to evaluate the trade-off between accuracy and computational overhead. To assess whether EPOS-VLM predicts actions that increase caption accuracy and semantic consistency, we fix the captioning architecture while varying the action policy: (i) Random Goal, which randomly samples navigation targets; (ii) Frontier Exploration [38], which prioritizes geometric coverage without semantic awareness; (iii) EPOS-VLM, which conditions actions on observations, explored map, and episodic memory. Metrics. We quantify caption accuracy using complementary captioning metrics [25]: BLEU-4 (B4) [19], METEOR (M) [3], ROUGE-L (RL) [15], CIDEr (CI) [31], and SPICE (SP) [2]. We use established implementations [6, 22], computing per object scores and averaging across test scenes. We evaluate cross-view semantic consistency through SBERT [23] embedding cosine similarity (CS), where higher values indicate greater consistency, supplemented by InterQuartile Range (IQR) for dispersion analysis. To isolate episodic memorys contribution, we compare the full model against stateless ablation (w/o memory) that processes only current RGB observations and explored maps. For data association, we use complementary object tracking metrics [33, 36, 42] to evaluate object identity correctness and temporal consistency during explorations: Association Accuracy (Acc) measures the proportion of correct identity assignments; F1-Match (F1-M) and F1-New (F1-N) measure the reliability in matching observations to existing objects or assigning new IDs respectively; Identity Switches (IDSW) [36] measures the amount of matches between an object identity and other existing objects, while Track Fragmentation (Frag) [33] the matches with new object identities.

sections.title: 5.1 Pseudo-Captioning Evaluation
sections.content: In Tab. 8 we compare our pseudo-captioner 3D-CPS with previous methods. 3D-CPS consistently outperforms all baselines in pseudo-captioning performance, showing that processing visual and language information in form of episodic 10 T.Galliena et al. Table 1: Pseudo-captioning performance on manually annotated HM3D [21] test set. Pseudo-captioner LAN RGB B4 RL CI SP CS IC3 [5] LD-CPS [8] ECO-score [13] 3D-CPS 1.36 13.56 15.17 0.03 11.87 57.89 21.02 26.86 53.10 1.36 35.81 73.00 18.36 23.27 47.32 1.30 34.98 72.16 28.54 31.97 61.13 1.92 44.73 78.26 KEYS LAN : language, RGB: image, B4: BLEU, : METEOR, RL: ROUGE-L, CI: CIDEr, SP : SPICE, CS: cosine similarity between SBERT embedding of prediction and annotation. memory during caption aggregation yields more accurate and semantically aligned object descriptions than baselines. Unlike IC3 [5], which summarizes captions assuming that all input views are correct, our approach filters out inconsistent observations, resulting in solid improvement across all metrics. Consequently, our method gains +32.86 percentage points (p.p.) in SPICE and +20.37 p.p. in caption similarity (CS). Compared to LD-CPS [8], which relies purely on language-based aggregation, our method leverages visual data to resolve semantic ambiguities, improving SPICE by 8.92 p.p. and CS by +5.26 p.p. Furthermore, while ECO-score [13] might select the wrong description based on the object images and captions alignment, 3D-CPS processes object images and captions to generate more comprehensive and accurate description, outperforming ECOscore by +0.62 in CI and +6.10 p.p. in CS. Based on this analysis, we supervise EPOS-VLM fine-tuning of the object-level captions with 3D-CPSs output.

sections.title: 5.2 Object-level Captioning and Semantic Consistency
sections.content: Captioning performance. In Tab. 2, we show the performance in object-level captioning evaluated on HM3D test set. EPOS-VLM outperforms all off-the-shelf image captioners, achieving +9.76 p.p. in SP and +0.89 in CI compared to the best performing VLM (Intern-VL [34]). This result highlights that despite state-of-the-art performance in image captioning, off-the-shelf VLMs are not tailored for embodied settings. EPOS-VLMs performance surpasses also Galliena et al.s multi-stage pipeline [8], with improvements of +0.65 in CI, +2.04 p.p. in SP, and +7.12 p.p. in CS. Compared to Galliena et al.s method, we use the full image frame instead of cropping objects, adding more context to the VLM input, and we use the episodic memory containing the correspondence between object instance and the captions from different viewpoints to disambiguate challenging viewpoints. We compare also EPOS-VLM full model against variant without episodic memory, which conditions only on the current observation and map. Removing memory prevents data association and forces the model to process each time step independently. This results in performance degradation of -14.15 p.p. in SP and -12.60 CS compared to the full model, confirming that episodic memory is core component of the proposed framework. Cross-View Semantic Consistency. Tab. 2 also shows cosine similarity across all captions generated for the same object instance and averaged over the test set. EPOS-VLM achieves the state-of-the-art performance in Mean CS, higher Memory-Augmented VisionLanguage Agents 11 Table 2: Caption accuracy (B4 CS) and caption consistency across multiple views of the same object (Mean CS IQR) on HM3D [21] manually annotated test set. Model Accuracy B4 RL CI SP CS Mean CS Median CS IQR Consistency 16.00 15.97 41.15 0.84 29.88 60.01 Qwen3-VL [39] 10.99 14.51 39.99 0.57 26.16 56.88 BLIP-2 [14] 17.21 16.87 46.02 0.98 30.02 61.18 Intern-VL [34] 15.23 16.13 43.33 0.83 29.95 57.47 CoCa [40] Florence [41] 14.19 17.26 44.03 0.78 31.45 59.12 Galliena et al. [8] 19.02 23.04 48.02 1.12 39.78 70.00 25.86 27.89 59.88 1.87 41.82 77.12 EPOS-VLM 16.65 18.01 45.34 0.85 27.67 64.62 w/o memory 59.65 60.15 55.19 57.87 52.56 81.98 89.37 52.12 60.01 64.12 58.76 57.26 55.00 83.07 90.32 52.84 29.12 25.29 27.34 29.01 26.98 8.87 2.79 32.01 KEYS B4: BLEU, : METEOR, RL: ROUGE-L, CI: CIDEr, SP : SPICE, CS: cosine similarity between SBERT embedding of prediction and annotation, Mean CS / Median CS: cosine similarity between SBERT embeddings of captions for the same object (higher, better), IQR: interquartile range of cosine similarity (lower, better). Table 3: Caption accuracy (B4CS) and caption consistency across multiple views of the same object (Mean CSIQR) on Gibson [37] manually annotated test set. Methods are trained on HM3D [21]. Model Accuracy B4 RL CI SP CS Mean CS Median CS IQR Consistency Galliena et al. [8] 17.03 19.65 46.88 1.05 35.58 66.03 19.88 22.94 53.87 1.63 39.14 75.86 EPOS-VLM 78.18 85.83 80.10 85.94 11.92 4.17 KEYS B4: BLEU-4, : METEOR, RL: ROUGE-L, CI: CIDEr, SP : SPICE, CS: cosine similarity between SBERT embedding of prediction and annotation. Mean CS / Median CS: cosine similarity between SBERT embeddings of captions for the same object (higher, better), IQR: interquartile range of cosine similarity (lower, better). than the best performing VLM (BLIP-2) by +29.22 p.p., while scoring the lowest IQR. High IQR in baselines (more than 25) reflects inconsistent object descriptions due to viewpoint changes or partial occlusions. In contrast, EPOS-VLM is trained to look at the same objects from different viewpoints, and progressively refine their descriptions. EPOS-VLM also outperforms the consistency-based method [8] by +7.39 p.p. in Mean CS and -6.08 in IQR. These results demonstrate that memorizing object-level captions and object positions during navigation increases consistency of object descriptions across viewpoint changes. The memory-ablated variant has lower scores than the full model confirming that consistency is direct result of the proposed episodic memory. Generalization to unseen environments. Tab. 3 compares the performance of EPOS-VLM with the method by Galliena et al. [8], both trained on HM3D and tested on Gibson [37], whose scenes differ substantially in layout and appearance. EPOS-VLM achieves higher caption accuracy with +0.58 CI and +3.46 p.p. SP, and improved alignment with the reference descriptions with +7.65 p.p. compared to the multi-stage method [8]. Crucially, EPOS-VLM also achieves higher semantic consistency in objects descriptions, with higher mean and median caption similarity and lower dispersion, compared to Galliena et al. [8]. Although Gibson represents challenging domain shift due to the different 12 T.Galliena et al. Table 4: Data association performance over exploration trajectories on HM3D [21] test set. Method Acc F1-M F1-N IDSW Frag Galliena et al. [8] EPOS-VLM 0.91 0.93 0.95 0.97 0.90 0.89 0.11 0.09 0.08 0.06 KEYS Acc: matching accuracy, 1 : F1-score for matching already observed objects, 1N : F1-score for detecting newly observed objects (NEW), IDSW : ID switches, rag: track fragmentation environments and lower mesh quality compared to HM3D, the performance gap remains moderate, indicating that the learned object-level memory and multi-view aggregation mechanisms transfer effectively to previously unseen environments. These results suggest that explicitly modeling episodic memory and enforcing semantic consistency across viewpoints leads to more robust and transferable object-level representations, which are essential for deploying embodied VisionLanguage agents in diverse real-world settings.

sections.title: 5.3 Data Association and Scalability
sections.content: Tab. 4 compares the performance between EPOS-VLM and point cloud based method used by Galliena et al. [8] in associating persistent object identifier to previously seen physical object or to new one based on visual observation. The results show that EPOS-VLM achieves comparable results with the point cloud based method with +0.02 p.p. in Acc, +0.02 p.p. in F1-M and -0.01 p.p. in F1-N. Despite similar performance, we represent objects using the estimated 3D position and track their change during the exploration (sparse representation), rather than high-resolution point cloud (dense representation) embedding also the visual and language embeddings. To analyse the advantages of our memory representation, we compare the inference time and memory usage at test time for EPOS-VLM and the point cloud baseline (see Fig. 3). The inference time of the point cloud baseline approximately exhibits linear inference time after 100 steps. In contrast, our method maintains near-constant inference time of 0.7s per step, attributable to leveraging fixed-capacity tokenized memory. Similarly, the memory footprint of EPOS-VLM remains below 10 kB, with the point-cloud baseline growing around 1 GB over the course of an episode. Together, these results suggest that data association for object captioning does not require dense, high-resolution object representation of the environment. Instead, our textual episodic memory containing sparse object representation, combined with model capable of reasoning over spatial and semantic context, achieves comparable performance while drastically reducing computational overhead and memory usage.

sections.title: 5.4 Action Policy Evaluation
sections.content: To quantitatively assess whether EPOS-VLM generates trajectories that enhance semantic consistency, we compare EPOS-VLM exploration policy with frontier Memory-Augmented VisionLanguage Agents 13 2.5 2 1. 1 0.5 ] [ i 0 ] [ m 103 106 109 0 400 200 Step number 300 400 100 200 Step number Fig. 3: Mean and standard deviation of inference time (left) and memory occupancy (right) averaged over HM3D [21] test episodes for EPOS-VLM and the dense point cloud baseline [8]. KEY: Point cloud, Ours Table 5: Caption accuracy (B4CS) and caption consistency across multiple views of the same object (Mean CSIQR) varying exploration policy on HM3D [21] manually annotated test set. Policy Accuracy B4 RL CI SP CS Mean CS Median CS IQR Consistency Random Goal 21.98 24.12 52.78 1.38 33.88 74.02 Frontier Exploration [38] 22.87 25.01 53.67 1.50 35.36 74.89 25.86 27.89 59.88 1.87 41.82 77.12 EPOS-VLM 80.33 80.21 89.37 80.65 81.03 90.32 9.03 8.45 2. KEYS B4: BLEU-4, : METEOR, RL: ROUGE-L, CI: CIDEr, SP : SPICE, CS: cosine similarity between SBERT embeddings of prediction and annotation. Mean CS and Median CS measure cross-view consistency (higher is better), IQR measures variability (lower is better). exploration [38] and random goal exploration. Results in Tab. 5 show that EPOSVLM consistently achieves significant improvements across all metrics. Specifically, EPOS-VLM outperforms Frontier Exploration by +0.39 p.p. in CI and +4.38 p.p. in SP. EPOS-VLM also yields an improvement in cross-view caption consistency, with gains of +9.22 p.p. in Mean CS and -6.14 in IQR. These improvements stem from EPOS-VLM different action selection: Frontier Exploration prioritizes geometric coverage without explicit mechanisms for resolving semantic ambiguity, whereas EPOS-VLM revisits objects exhibiting inconsistent captions. Overall, our analysis confirms that semantic consistency is not merely function of observation aggregation and memory retention, but critically depends on action policies that increase semantic consistency.

sections.title: 5.5 Qualitative results
sections.content: Fig. 4 compares qualitatively object captions predicted by Qwen3-VL and EPOSVLM across different views during exploration. Qwen3-VL predicts independent captions for each object crop, resulting in semantic inconsistency e.g., alternating sofa, couch and armchair for the same object. In contrast, our model leverages explicit memory and data association to maintain persistent object identity across sequential views and to iteratively refine descriptions while preserving core 14 T.Galliena et al. Fig. 4: Comparison of object captions predicted by VLM baseline (Qwen3-VL) and our method along successive viewpoints of an exploration. Mistakes highlighted in red. attributes, yielding compact, object-level captions resilient to viewpoint variation, occlusion, and scale changes. More qualitative results in Supp. Mat.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: This work introduced EPOS-VLM, memory-augmented Vision-Language-Action model for embodied captioning, addressing caption consistency of the same objects. EPOS-VLM integrates object-level episodic memory, object descriptions association, and action selection in single autoregressive architecture. This enables the model to process the history of the objects locations and descriptions across time and viewpoints. Extensive experiments demonstrate significant improvements in object-level caption accuracy, cross-view semantic consistency, long-horizon data association, and computational scalability compared to prior methods. This supports the idea that long-horizon semantic reasoning in embodied agents requires persistent memory as opposed to transient visual representation. Limitations and future work. EPOS-VLM uses an external instance segmentation model that can predict inaccurate object masks causing mistakes in data association and captioning; the experimental validation focused on analysing the performance in photorealistic and static environments assuming ideal sensors. Future work will integrate the segmentation phase inside the model, address sensor noise and real-time constraints during real-world deployment, and explore the capabilities of our memory in dynamic scenes, where new objects may appear.

EPOS-VLM details A.1 Architecture In all our experiments, we use pretrained Qwen3-VL-2B [39] model as the basis for EPOS-VLM. The vocabulary is extended with the addition of the following non-learnable special tokens: [SCENE-START], [SCENE-END], [OBJ-ID], [POS], [CAP-HISTORY], [CAPTION], [ACTION], [MATCH]. These are processed by the standard tokenizer and are used by the model to have structured representation of the episodic object memory introduced in Sec. 3.2. A.2 Prompt Formatting This section details how the visual and textual input modalities are constructed and serialized before being passed to the Unified VLM at each timestep. Combined Visual Input. The RGB-D observation It and the explored map Et are resized to dimensions 224 224 3, horizontally stacked to form an input image. The RGB image is augmented with object-level annotations. Bounding boxes detected by the object detector are drawn on the RGB image. Each bounding box is also labeled on the image with random transient ID rjKt , where Kt is the number of detected objects in the image. The explored map shows the areas of the map that have been explored up to time step t, as well as the agent current position and field of view. Textual Prompt. The textual input includes prompt with instructions followed by the serialized episodic memory Mt1. The episodic object memory is formatted as sequence of object data blocks delimited by special tokens and injected into the language context as described in Sec. 3.2. This representation provides the model with compact, object-centric summary of past observations, including spatial location and accumulated semantic evidence. The following is an example of textual input: Prompt example. [TASK-START] Your task is object linking and action prediction. You are given: - MEMORY of previously seen objects, each with fixed OBJ-ID. - FRAME with current objects, each having temporary OBJ-ID (a random ID drawn over the image). For each object in the FRAME, decide whether it corresponds to one MEMORY object. If it matches, output: [MATCH] <frame_random_id> : <memory_obj_id> If it is new object, output: [MATCH] <frame_random_id> : NEW_ID Memory-Augmented VisionLanguage Agents 19 After matching all objects, predict the action to take: [ACTION] [ACTION] Available actions are: move_forward, stop, turn_left, turn_right Below are the FRAME objects and their random IDs: 37, 16 [SCENE-START] [OBJ-ID] 11 [CAPTION-HISTORY] 2: "a bed with pink and blue polka dot sheet." [POSITION] [-7.09, 1.67, 3.79] [OBJ-ID] 12 [CAPTION-HISTORY] 1: "a black leather couch with white wall in the background." 1: "a black leather chair in room with white wall." 1: "a black leather couch with white pillow on it." 1: "a black leather couch with pillow on it." 3: "a black leather couch in living room." [POSITION] [-2.91, 1.56, 2.59] [SCENE-END] A.3 Output Parsing. The model generates structured autoregressive sequence containing data association decisions, object captions, and the navigation action. The sequence is parsed by identifying the special tokens [MATCH], [CAPTION], and [ACTION] and extracting the corresponding arguments. The [MATCH] token encodes the data association between objects detected in the current frame and objects stored in episodic memory. Specifically, it follows the format [MATCH] rj : oid,o, where rj is the transient random identifier assigned to the j-th detection in the current frame at timestep t, and oid,o is the persistent object identifier stored in episodic memory. If the detection corresponds to previously unseen object, the model emits NEW_ID instead of memory identifier. For example, consider the following generated output: [MATCH] 37 : 12 [MATCH] 16 : NEW_ID [CAPTION] "a black leather couch with white pillow" [CAPTION] "a small wooden side table" [ACTION] move_forward The parsing procedure extracts: T.Galliena et al. Data associations: 37 12, 16 NEW_ID Object captions predicted for the current detections Navigation action: move_forward The data association updates the episodic memory by linking detections to existing object entries or creating new ones when NEW_ID is emitted. Predicted captions are added to the caption history of the corresponding object, and the predicted action is executed by the navigation controller.

sections.title: B Data Collection Details
sections.content: This section provides detailed description of the trajectory collection procedure used to build the training dataset. Object detection and caption prediction. At each time step, the RGB observation is processed using Mask2Former to generate instance segmentation masks and corresponding bounding boxes. Each detected instance is assigned transient, randomly sampled identifier, reinitialized independently per frame and explicitly decoupled from persistent object identities oid,o. This design precludes reliance on temporal identity continuity, thereby mitigating the risk of shortcut learning via ID memorization during training. For each bounding box, an object-centric crop is extracted and processed by pretrained image captioning model (Qwen3-VL-2B [39]) to produce descriptive caption. The caption is subsequently encoded into fixed-dimensional text embedding using SentenceTransformer (SBERT) [22] model. This embedding serves as the input for the following disagreement map construction module. Disagreement map construction. As in [8], disagreement is defined as semantic inconsistency between all captions predicted for the same object. Following [8], the agent maintains spatial disagreement map over the navigable area of the environment, defined in the top-down map frame. Intuitively, each map cell accumulates score reflecting semantic instability associated with observations projected into that region over time. In practice, this is implemented by aggregating caption embedding statistics over the reconstructed point cloud and projecting the resulting disagreement values into the map coordinate system using Habitats world-to-map transformation. Target object selection. The disagreement map is normalized and thresholded to isolate high-disagreement areas. 2D connected components is used to cluster the thresholded disagreement into target regions; regions with an area below threshold Amin are discarded since they might be artifacts or represent only part of an object. Each target region is represented by 2D centroid. Target regions are then ranked by disagreement and processed in descending order. Candidate viewpoint generation. For each cluster, we generate candidate viewpoints around its boundary. Candidate viewpoints are selected at Nr radial distances in the set from the target object and oriented towards towards the centroid. Each candidate viewpoint is filtered using navigability check with safety margin to ensure the camera is not placed too close to obstacles. Finally, Memory-Augmented VisionLanguage Agents 21 Table 6: Parameters used during trajectory collection. Symbol Description Value Weighting coefficient for viewpoint selection 0.7 α Minimum area of high-disagreement regions 100 pixels Amin Viewpoint sampling radii Candidate viewpoints per radius Nr Nmin Minimum viewpoints per target object Nmax Maximum viewpoints per target object τs ϵp Nrec Sliding window for stuck detection Minimum displacement threshold Maximum recovery attempts {0.5, 1.0, 2.0} 30 5 20 5 steps 0.15 5 random number of viewpoints between Nmin and Nmax is selected from the candidates. Viewpoints selection. Viewpoints are ranked using heuristic that combines the target objects disagreement, weighted by coefficient α, and the travel distance cost from the agents position to the viewpoint, weighted by 1 α. Viewpoints are then sent as goals to the agent from the highest ranking to the lowest. α was empirically chosen to balance the agent visiting high disagreement viewpoints first while also prioritizing nearby objects. Navigation. The agent navigates to each viewpoint using the shortest path planner provided by Habitat sim [26]. At each timestep the disagreement statistics are updated for all the objects present in the current field of view of the agent. Failure recovery. failure recovery policy detects when the agent becomes stuck while navigating toward goal, ensuring that no exploration steps are lost. This condition is identified by monitoring the displacement of the agent over sliding window of τs steps. If the displacement remains below threshold ϵp, the agent is considered stuck. When this condition is detected, recovery behaviour is triggered by sampling candidate target positions within small radius around the current agent position and attempting to navigate toward them. Candidate positions are randomly generated until the agent resumes motion. If the agent fails to recover after Nrec attempts, the current target object is discarded and the agent proceeds to the next one. The numerical values of the parameters τs, ϵp, and Nrec are reported in Table 6. Experimental configuration parameters. Tab. 6 reports summary of the parameters and values used for data collection. LoRA Fine-Tuning To efficiently fine-tune the pretrained VisionLanguage backbone, we adopt LowRank Adaptation (LoRA) [11] as parameter-efficient training strategy. Rather than updating all model parameters, LoRA injects trainable low-rank matrices into selected linear layers, significantly reducing memory footprint and training cost while preserving performance. 22 T.Galliena et al. Table 7: LoRA configuration used for fine-tuning the Unified VLM. Parameter Value Rank (r) Scaling factor (α) 8 Dropout 16 Target modules 0.05 q_proj, k_proj, v_proj, o_proj, language_model.embed_tokens, lm_head e y e 4,000 2,000 0 20 15 10 5 0 e o r u 400 0 100 200 Step number 300 400 100 200 Step number 300 Fig. 5: Mean and standard deviation of memory token count (left) and number of objects (right) per step, averaged over all HM3D test episodes. We apply LoRA to both the attention projections and selected language modeling components of the VLM. The specific configuration used in all experiments is summarized in Table 7. This configuration allows the model to adapt both its multimodal attention mechanisms and its output language distribution, which is critical for jointly learning data association, object-centric captioning, and action prediction within unified sequence modeling framework.

sections.title: D Additional results
sections.content: D.1 Memory Scalability To assess the scalability of the proposed memory representation, we quantify the evolution of memory token count throughout episodic exploration as novel objects are discovered and tracked. Fig. 5 shows the progression of memory tokens and the object detected during the exploration. The mean memory token count exhibits similar increasing trend compared to the number of actively tracked objects. This indicates that tokenization scales predominantly with object cardinality, rather than trajectory duration. The absence of abrupt deviations from object count confirms that episodic memory remains structurally compact and organized during exploration. Furthermore, the number of memory tokens has low inter-episode variance, underscoring consistent performance across diverse scenes and trajectories. Memory-Augmented VisionLanguage Agents 23 (a) (b) (c) (d) Fig. 6: Examples of episodic trajectories on scenes from the HM3D test set. (a),(b),(c): the agent successfully revisits one or more objects from different viewpoints, while avoiding obstacles. (d): partial navigation failure where the agent hits wall. These results provide empirical validation that our tokenization scheme yields predictably bounded memory representation, in direct contrast to geometrycentric mapping approaches (e.g., dense point cloud accumulation), which inherently suffer from unbounded memory expansion. D.2 Qualitative results Figure 6 shows some qualitative examples of exploration trajectories on scenes from the HM3D test set. Figures 6(a,b,c) depict typical exploration episode in which the agent revisits one or more objects multiple times from different vantage points. The agent mostly focuses on one or two objects per episode due to the episode duration (400 steps). In Figures 6d the agent, while still revisiting an object in the lower left corner of the map, collides with nearby wall, therefore some of the steps are spent on recovering from the collision. Figure 7 qualitatively compares randomly chosen object captions predicted by the vanilla Qwen3-VL model and the fine-tuned EPOS-VLM across multiple views observed during exploration. The vanilla model predicts captions independently for each crop, often producing view-dependent descriptions or minor semantic variations for the same object. In contrast, EPOS-VLM leverages episodic memory and explicit data association to preserve object identity across observations, resulting in more semantically consistent and accurate captions across viewpoints compared to Qwen3-VL-2B. 24 T.Galliena et al. Fig. 7: Additional qualitative examples for the vanilla and fine-tuned model of captions predicted over three views of the same object. Errors are highlighted in red. Table 8: Pseudo-captioning performance on manually annotated HM3D [21] test set. VLM B4 RL CI SP InternVL3.5-30B [34] 27.76 30.11 61.06 1.87 44.53 Gemma3-27B [30] 26.54 29.12 58.95 1.68 42.06 Qwen3-VL-30B [39] 28.54 31.97 61.13 1.92 44.73 CS 76.19 73.36 78.26 KEYS B4: BLEU, : METEOR, RL: ROUGE-L, CI: CIDEr, SP : SPICE, CS: cosine similarity between SBERT embedding of prediction and annotation. Additional pseudo-captioner comparisons To motivate the choice of Qwen3-VL-30B for the proposed 3D-CPS pseudocaptioner, we additionally evaluate it against two large vision-language models with similar number of parameters on the manually annotated HM3D test set (Tab. 8). Overall, all models achieve strong results, indicating that the proposed pseudo-captioning strategy is valid across different VLM backbones. Among them, Qwen3-VL-30B achieves the best performance across all metrics, predicting pseudo-captions that are both more accurate with respect to human annotations and more semantically consistent. Based on these results, we adopt Qwen3-VL30B as the backbone for 3D-CPS.
